cards.text: Ref qualifiers allow you to overload member functions based on whether the implicit object is an {{c1::lvalue}} or {{c2::rvalue}}.
To specify that a member function can only be called on lvalues, add {{c1::&}} after the function signature: void func() {{c1::&}}
To specify that a member function can only be called on rvalues, add {{c1::&&}} after the function signature: void func() {{c1::&&}}
Ref qualifiers are placed {{c1::after}} the const qualifier but {{c2::before}} the function body or trailing return type.

cards.front: What is the purpose of ref qualifiers?
cards.back: To allow different behavior when a member function is called on an lvalue (persistent object) versus an rvalue (temporary object).

cards.front: Can you have both lvalue and rvalue overloads of the same member function?
cards.back: Yes, you can provide separate implementations: one with & for lvalues and one with && for rvalues.

cards.question: Which version of getValue() will be called for each case?
cards.explanation: d is an lvalue, so the & version is called. Data() is a temporary rvalue, so the && version is called.

cards.question: Complete the syntax for this lvalue-qualified member function.
What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Cannot mix ref-qualified and non-ref-qualified overloads of the same function.
cards.explanation: If you provide ref-qualifiers for one overload, all overloads must have ref-qualifiers.

Ref qualifiers are useful when:
  • You want to return a reference from lvalues but a value from rvalues
  • Preventing dangerous operations on temporaries
  • Optimizing temporary object handling
  • Common use case: preventing assignment to temporaries
examples: int operator[](int i) && { return m_data[i]; }  // Return by value for rvalues
